text: <@U02U0AS3J49> that's actually a much more convincing case for being able to disassemble terms! I felt that there is value in what Tylr is demoing, but that their example is not the most convincing one. Now the question is, how often do you actually need to move root and one child, leaving the other behind..

elements.text: that's actually a much more convincing case for being able to disassemble terms! I felt that there is value in what Tylr is demoing, but that their example is not the most convincing one. Now the question is, how often do you actually need to move root and one child, leaving the other behind..

text: Of course, it could be that being able to disassemble improves interactions a bit here and there, although there's no killer case for it.

elements.text: Of course, it could be that being able to disassemble improves interactions a bit here and there, although there's no killer case for it.

text: Yes. More than that, I'm not sure you couldn't make a block-based editor work that way. If CTRL+drag allowed you to place a term outside another term instead of inside it, changing what qualifies as a place you can drop it, you could drop *4 on top of whatever you like. You could even have three modes, one for matching external only (the default now), one for matching internal only, and one for matching both. But again... who needs it? Anyone? I don't know.

elements.text: Yes. More than that, I'm not sure you couldn't make a block-based editor work that way. text: Have any of you tried fossil? I haven't tried it yet, but if the claims on their site are true, it sounds like it's more well-suited than git for most people's personal projects: <https://www.fossil-scm.org/home/doc/trunk/www/fossil-v-git.wiki|https://www.fossil-scm.org/home/doc/trunk/www/fossil-v-git.wiki>

elements.text: Have any of you tried fossil? I haven't tried it yet, but if the claims on their site are true, it sounds like it's more well-suited than git for most people's personal projects:

text:
I am running a small document server plus task management system for my condo as a Fossil repository. Trying out Fossil was one motivation, the simplicity of the setup another one. Fossil contains a Web server and an issue tracker, all in a single executable, so that's all I need to run on my little server.
But I haven't used Fossil for code so far. The main reason is that it doesn't have decent Emacs integration. Git has Magit, which is the best UI I have ever seen for a VCS. I'd rather fight git now and then than give up Magit.
